some_term_is_stuck.solution:
Example some_term_is_stuck :
  exists t, stuck t.
Proof.
  exists <{ succ true }>. unfold stuck. split.
  - unfold step_normal_form, normal_form. intros [t' H]. inversion H. subst. inversion H1.
  - intros H. inversion H.
    + inversion H0.
    + inversion H0. inversion H1.
Qed.
some_term_is_stuck.explanation: succ true is stuck: it's not a value (not nvalue or bvalue) and can't step (succ requires a numeric argument to step).

value_is_nf.solution:
Lemma value_is_nf : forall t,
  value t -> step_normal_form t.
Proof.
  intros t Hv. unfold step_normal_form, normal_form.
  intros [t' Hstep].
  inversion Hv.
  - (* bvalue *) destruct H; inversion Hstep.
  - (* nvalue *) induction H.
    + inversion Hstep.
    + apply IHnvalue. inversion Hstep. subst. exists t1'. assumption.
      right. assumption.
Qed.
value_is_nf.explanation: Values cannot step. Boolean values (tru/fls) trivially can't step. Numeric values are shown by induction: 0 can't step, succ nv can't step because nv can't step (by IH).

step_deterministic.explanation: Determinism follows from value_is_nf: conflicting cases where a value also steps lead to contradictions.

succ_hastype_nat__hastype_nat.solution:
Example succ_hastype_nat__hastype_nat : forall t,
  <{ |--  succ t \in Nat }> ->
  <{ |-- t \in Nat }>.
Proof.
  intros t H. inversion H. assumption.
Qed.
succ_hastype_nat__hastype_nat.explanation: By inverting the typing derivation of succ t : Nat, we get t : Nat.

finish_progress.solution:
Theorem progress : forall t T,
  <{ |-- t \in T }> ->
  value t \/ exists t', t --> t'.
Proof.
  intros t T HT.
  induction HT; auto.
  - (* T_If *)
    right. destruct IHHT1.
    + apply (bool_canonical t1 HT1) in H.
      destruct H.
      * exists t2. auto.
      * exists t3. auto.
    + destruct H as [t1' H1].
      exists <{ if t1' then t2 else t3 }>. auto.
  - (* T_Succ *)
    destruct IHHT.
    + left. right. apply nv_succ. apply (nat_canonical t1 HT). assumption.
    + right. destruct H as [t1' H1]. exists <{ succ t1' }>. auto.
  - (* T_Pred *)
    right. destruct IHHT.
    + apply (nat_canonical t1 HT) in H. destruct H.
      * exists <{ 0 }>. auto.
      * exists t. auto.
    + destruct H as [t1' H1]. exists <{ pred t1' }>. auto.
  - (* T_Iszero *)
    right. destruct IHHT.
    + apply (nat_canonical t1 HT) in H. destruct H.
      * exists <{ true }>. auto.
      * exists <{ false }>. auto.
    + destruct H as [t1' H1]. exists <{ iszero t1' }>. auto.
Qed.
finish_progress.explanation: For succ: if t1 is a value, it's a nat (by canonical forms), so succ t1 is a value. If t1 steps, succ steps too. Similar for pred and iszero.

finish_preservation.solution:
Theorem preservation : forall t t' T,
  <{ |-- t \in T }> ->
  t --> t' ->
  <{ |-- t' \in T }>.
Proof.
  intros t t' T HT HE.
  generalize dependent t'.
  induction HT;
       intros t' HE;
       try solve_by_invert.
    - (* T_If *) inversion HE; subst; clear HE.
      + assumption.
      + assumption.
      + apply T_If; try assumption. apply IHHT1; assumption.
    - (* T_Succ *) inversion HE; subst. apply T_Succ. apply IHHT. assumption.
    - (* T_Pred *) inversion HE; subst.
      + apply T_Zero.
      + inversion HT. assumption.
      + apply T_Pred. apply IHHT. assumption.
    - (* T_Iszero *) inversion HE; subst.
      + apply T_True.
      + apply T_False.
      + apply T_Iszero. apply IHHT. assumption.
Qed.
finish_preservation.explanation: By induction on the typing derivation. For each case, invert the step relation and apply the IH or direct typing rules.

preservation_alternate_proof.solution:
Theorem preservation' : forall t t' T,
  <{ |-- t \in T }> ->
  t --> t' ->
  <{ |-- t' \in T }>.
Proof with eauto.
  intros t t' T HT HE.
  generalize dependent T.
  induction HE; intros T HT; inversion HT; subst...
  - (* ST_Pred_Succ *) inversion H1. assumption.
Qed.
preservation_alternate_proof.explanation: By induction on the step derivation instead of the typing derivation. Most cases are handled by eauto after inverting the typing.

subject_expansion.solution:
Theorem subject_expansion:
  (forall t t' T, t --> t' /\ <{ |-- t' \in T }> -> <{ |-- t \in T }>)
  \/
  ~ (forall t t' T, t --> t' /\ <{ |-- t' \in T }> -> <{ |-- t \in T }>).
Proof.
  right. intros H.
  assert (Hstep : <{ if true then 0 else false }> --> <{ 0 }>).
  { apply ST_IfTrue. }
  assert (Hty : <{ |-- 0 \in Nat }>). { apply T_Zero. }
  assert (H1 : <{ |-- if true then 0 else false \in Nat }>).
  { apply H. split; assumption. }
  inversion H1. subst. inversion H7.
Qed.
subject_expansion.explanation: Subject expansion fails: if true then 0 else false steps to 0 : Nat, but the original term is ill-typed because false : Bool <> Nat.

variation1.solution:
(* With T_SuccBool: |-- t : Bool -> |-- succ t : Bool *)
(* Determinism: remains true (step rules unchanged) *)
(* Progress: remains true (succ of bool value is now a bool value? No - succ true is still not a value in the step relation. Actually progress BECOMES FALSE because succ true : Bool but succ true is stuck) *)
(* Preservation: remains true (typing is preserved through steps) *)
variation1.explanation: Adding T_SuccBool breaks progress because succ true would have type Bool but is stuck (not a value, can't step).

variation2.solution:
(* With T_SuccBool removed and ST_Funny1 added: succ true --> true *)
(* Determinism: remains true *)
(* Progress: remains true *)
(* Preservation: becomes false (succ true : Nat steps to true : Bool) *)
variation2.explanation: ST_Funny1 breaks preservation because succ true has type Nat but steps to true which has type Bool.

variation3.solution:
(* With ST_Pred_true and ST_Pred_false rules *)
(* Progress: remains true *)
(* Preservation: becomes false *)
variation3.explanation: pred true has type Nat but would step in a way that breaks type preservation.

variation4.solution:
(* With additional rule making succ pred step differently *)
(* Evaluation changes affect determinism and possibly preservation. *)
variation4.explanation: Analyze which properties (determinism, progress, preservation) hold or fail with the modified evaluation rules.

variation5.solution:
(* With T_Iszero_Nat added: if |-- t : Nat then |-- iszero t : Nat *)
(* Preservation: becomes false (iszero (succ 0) : Nat but steps to false : Bool) *)
variation5.explanation: Adding this typing rule breaks preservation because iszero reduces to a boolean, not a natural.

variation6.solution:
(* With an extra reduction rule *)
(* Analyze determinism, progress, and preservation for the specific variation. *)
variation6.explanation: Evaluate which safety properties hold or fail with the given evaluation rule modification.

more_variations.explanation: Design variations to the type system or step relation and analyze which metatheoretic properties survive.

remove_pred0.solution:
(* Manual grade exercise about removing the pred 0 --> 0 rule. *)
(* Without ST_Pred_Zero, pred 0 becomes stuck. Progress fails because pred 0 : Nat but can't step and isn't a value. *)
remove_pred0.explanation: Removing ST_Pred_Zero breaks progress for the term pred 0, which has type Nat but becomes stuck.

prog_pres_bigstep.solution:
(* Advanced manual grade exercise about progress and preservation for big-step semantics. *)
(* Big-step evaluation inherently only relates well-formed evaluations, so preservation holds trivially. Progress is essentially normalization. *)
prog_pres_bigstep.explanation: In big-step semantics, preservation holds by construction since evaluation only produces values. Progress corresponds to showing every well-typed term evaluates to some value.

finish_progress_informal.solution:
(** _Theorem_: If [|-- t \in T], then either [t] is a value or else
    [t --> t'] for some [t']. *)

(** _Proof_: By induction on a derivation of [|-- t \in T].

    - If the last rule in the derivation is [T_If], then [t = if t1
      then t2 else t3], with [|-- t1 \in Bool], [|-- t2 \in T] and [|-- t3
      \in T].  By the IH, either [t1] is a value or else [t1] can step
      to some [t1'].

      - If [t1] is a value, then by the canonical forms lemmas
        and the fact that [|-- t1 \in Bool] we have that [t1]
        is a [bvalue] -- i.e., it is either [true] or [false].
        If [t1 = true], then [t] steps to [t2] by [ST_IfTrue],
        while if [t1 = false], then [t] steps to [t3] by
        [ST_IfFalse].  Either way, [t] can step, which is what
        we wanted to show.

      - If [t1] itself can take a step, then, by [ST_If], so can
        [t].

    - (* FILL IN HERE *)
 *)

finish_preservation_informal.solution:
(** _Theorem_: If [|-- t \in T] and [t --> t'], then [|-- t' \in T]. *)

(** _Proof_: By induction on a derivation of [|-- t \in T].

    - If the last rule in the derivation is [T_If], then [t = if t1
      then t2 else t3], with [|-- t1 \in Bool], [|-- t2 \in T] and [|-- t3
      \in T].

      Inspecting the rules for the small-step reduction relation and
      remembering that [t] has the form [if ...], we see that the
      only ones that could have been used to prove [t --> t'] are
      [ST_IfTrue], [ST_IfFalse], or [ST_If].

      - If the last rule was [ST_IfTrue], then [t' = t2].  But we
        know that [|-- t2 \in T], so we are done.

      - If the last rule was [ST_IfFalse], then [t' = t3].  But we
        know that [|-- t3 \in T], so we are done.

      - If the last rule was [ST_If], then [t' = if t1' then t2
        else t3], where [t1 --> t1'].  We know [|-- t1 \in Bool] so,
        by the IH, [|-- t1' \in Bool].  The [T_If] rule then gives us
        [|-- if t1' then t2 else t3 \in T], as required.

    - (* FILL IN HERE *)
*)
